The two were lucky Sunwoo was letting them have a role in the plan. But if they really were committed to ending this for Solar, they'd have to know how to handle themselves and do their job properly. 

So, he had rose to his feet, making his way out of the room and towards the balcony. He had a pistol with a suppressor on it, so it wouldn't make any noise, and he figured he would just teach them to shoot some kind of object he'd set out on the ledge. 

It would be nothing compared to the in depth training he once went through as only a young child, but surely they wouldn't be completely hopeless. Sunwoo felt like he'd been shooting guns since he was five - well, because he had - he couldn't imagine it not coming naturally. 

Solar had watched him leave the room, before she looked to the two boys. 

"Uh, does he - want us to follow him?" Jinhwan asked no one in particular. 

"Yeah, that was his invitation," Sangyeon was the first to inform, "I suggest you don't keep him waiting, it's dire that you're both comfortable with a weapon before Friday comes."

"I don't think I need much practice," Jungkook hummed confidently, "I don't know about this idiot, though."

"Oh, right, because you're clearly an expert," Jinhwan scoffed, not caring to deal with Jungkook and his annoying mouth as he rose to his feet. The two boys had somewhat learned to tolerate each other for the sake of Solar, but it was still difficult. 

"Just go, both of you," the brunette had urged them like a lecturing parent, "think of it as something fun, you're getting lessons from a trained professional!"

She spoke about it so cheerily, it gave them both chills how casual this was to her. But they understood, they understood where she came from and it made sense. 

So, the two boys had hesitantly made their way to the balcony. The air was cold outside when they did so, a slight wind slapping them both in the face. Stepping out fully, Sunwoo was there, leaning against the brick wall of the apartment building, cigarette in between his fingers as he blew smoke into the air in front of them. 

His eyes didn't move from where he was staring off in the sky. He'd barely moved as he pulled something out of his pocket, a gun, before he chunked it onto the table that was in the middle of the balcony. The weapon hit the glass table with a slam, causing the two boys to exchange glances with each other. 

Jungkook had stepped forward to pick up the weapon, having a good idea as to what Sunwoo wanted them to do. He'd set out some bottles across the fencing of the balcony, and it didn't take a genius to guess they were meant to shoot them.

"This is a nice gun," he'd complimented as he looked down at the shiny weapon, he could only imagine how much it costed. 

"Custom," Sunwoo monotoned, "first gun I ever made when I was thirteen, upgraded it myself over the years." He blew more smoke into the air, before motioning to the ledge where the bottles sat, "it's important that you both at least understand how to shoot - but also hit." 

Jinhwan watched as Jungkook had stepped back to an appropriate distance, raising the weapon. He found himself wondering the obvious, "do you think a fight's going to break out, that he's going to bring men with him?"

The gang leader didn't look at either of them still, though he no doubt thought it was a dumb question. "You must prepare for every possible outcome," he deadpanned, chunking his cigarette off the side of the balcony, "even if the chance is 1, we need to be prepared."

Jungkook knew enough about weapons just from movies and video games. He cleared his throat, closing one eye as he loaded the gun, before aiming towards the first bottle. 

And he shot, taking it out swiftly. The bottle shattered into pieces, before falling somewhere below - and hopefully not hitting anyone. He found himself impressed at how fun it had felt, the gun shot so silently with little kickback. He couldn't imagine what it took to build your own weapon, but Sunwoo clearly knew what he was doing when he did so. 

"Not bad," Sunwoo hummed, but his voice was as blank as ever. The boy crossed his arms over his chest as he finally looked to Jungkook, "it's important to put into perspective the difference in distance it'll be. You'll be much further away, possibly on a roof, even." 

"So, like a lookout team?" Jungkook rose a brow, asking for confirmation. 

Sunwoo only nodded once. "Suppose you could call it that, it's impractical to have either of you on the front line, and dangerous to be anywhere near Solar," he explained with a sigh, "it's the safest if you're both somewhere high or far still within sight of the target, so if anything goes haywire, you shoot."

His instructions weren't that clear, but they were straightforward. Jungkook had thought about it for a second, before agreeing, "that sounds easy enough."

But Jinhwan felt insecure about the entire thing. He wanted to help, he swore to Solar that he could kill him for everything he had done to her, but after he felt the feeling of murder, he was still shaken up. He was still processing that someone had died by his hands. 

"How do we know if we should shoot?" 

Sunwoo had the most blank yet unimpressed look on his face when he looked to Jinhwan. After a second, a deep sigh had left his lips, "presumably if they shoot any of us," he explained slowly, as if he was talking to a child, "furthermore, if you see signs of them itching towards a weapon. They may have one hidden in a pocket, bag, wherever."

"Course," Jungkook hummed, "shoot them first type of deal."

Sunwoo only made a noise of agreement. Silence fell over the three as Jungkook turned to the short boy beside him, holding out the gun as he glared down at him. Jinhwan met his glare with one just as harsh, snatching it out of his hand. He still felt uneasy, especially with the feeling of a cold weapon, but he knew he couldn't show signs of weakness around Sunwoo nor Jungkook. 

Jungkook had watched him, raising a brow expectantly. He figured Jinhwan would fail or refuse to even fire it, just because of how he was. Even though he had decided that fighting with Jinhwan wasn't worth it, he still thought about when he rose that gun to his head. If you asked him a month ago if he hated Jinhwan enough to kill him, he would've said no, but at this point - he wasn't so sure. He couldn't stand him, the way he did what he did, told Solar such hateful things all because she protected herself, tried to manipulate her into thinking he did nothing wrong for so long. And now, he was forgiven, like he never did a thing - all because he probably gave her a half assed apology and claimed he was going to "help." 

It royally pissed him off. As Jinhwan had held the gun up in the direction of the bottles, swaying his head a bit to get his hair out of his face, Jungkook scoffed, turning away to focus on anything that wasn't the pathetic display he was about to see. 

That was when he thought of something - Sunwoo probably had no idea about what Jinhwan had said, about everything that had went down at the company in total. He figured Solar had yet to catch him up on that, and that when she did, she'd likely leave out the part that had to do with her ex boyfriend calling her a monster. 

The sound of breaking bottles was heard behind him, and Sunwoo had been blankly watching, speaking up to give him tips every now and then and explain what he should be looking for or doing. Jungkook debated on if he should ask Solar first, before he realized that she would be opposed to it no matter what. 

Usually, he'd never do something like this, but he felt like Sunwoo should know. 

"Alright, that's enough for now," Sunwoo had deadpanned as Jinhwan had shot the last bottle, "we can do some more - realistic practicing tomorrow, now that I have a sense of what you both need to work on."

"Sure, just tell Solar to let me know if I'm not here," Jinhwan had attested, before he disappeared back inside the apartment. 

The gang leader didn't move nor say anything more. He just stood there, leaning against the wall, taking out another cigarette and lighting it. His brown eyes stared at the sky still, like he was intently looking for something in the stars - possibly an answer, a sign, anything. Then, he'd blow smoke into the air, before deeply exhaling and taking another hit.

He looked so lacking of any feeling, yet he had to be feeling so much. Jungkook couldn't imagine what it felt like to have so much on your plate, to do such a dangerous and illegal job, knowing you could get caught at any second. He didn't know what it truly felt like to kill someone, and he probably never would. He wondered if that baggage ever followed Sunwoo around, if he ever regretted any of it. There had to be some emotion inside of him, after all, he clearly loved and cared more for Solar than any of them.

It was hard for him to say that, but it was true. Sunwoo would do anything for her, and he had. 

"Whatever you want to tell me, I suggest you spit it out."

He didn't expect him to say anything. Jungkook felt like a dumb ass, just standing there, so he cleared his throat and leaned against the wall on the other side of the door from where Sunwoo was. He hadn't thought much about what he wanted to say, but he figured he better just get it out. 

"Did Mi tell you about what exactly happened at the company the day everything happened?" 

There was a look of question in his eyes when he looked to the doe boy. "Haven't had the chance to catch up exactly, no, I found out on my own after I got discharged," he explained, blowing more smoke into the air, "though, I'm assuming you have some details for me I didn't know about."

Pursing his lips, he asked himself again if he wanted to tell him. Whether Solar liked it or not, he felt like he should. 

"Soomi and Jinhwan had a pretty vile fight afterwards," he blurted before he could disagree with himself again, but managed to keep his tone confident and calm, "Soomi killed Jimin, who was the girl he cheated on her with, she was working with her old manager the entire time."

Sunwoo likely already knew about who Jimin was and that Solar killed her. But the fight was news to him, judging from the way he looked to Jungkook with an intrigued expression, "I really hope you're not about to tell me he was upset that she killed his side piece."

"I'd say it was worse than that," Jungkook hummed, "he said some fucked up shit, called her a monster, said she was the sickest person he knew, just insane shit..." he trailed off with a deep exhale, glad to finally get it off his chest, "I couldn't imagine saying anything like that to her, to anyone who was defending themselves."

The gang leader was dead silent. He'd stood there, leaning against that brick wall, narrowing his eyes ahead of them at the darkness, deep in contemplation. It was clear he was angry, that it was building and boiling in his veins with each second that passed. Jungkook felt intimidated by Sunwoo, he always would because he knew what he was capable of. He could only imagine what he was thinking of right then, as he'd squeezed his cigarette so hard it crumbled underneath them on the ground. 

After a long while, Sunwoo let out a singular brief exhale, grabbing his gun from the table and heading for the door. "I appreciate the information," was all he said as he swung open the balcony door, heading inside the apartment. 

Jungkook would've felt bad if he didn't hate Jinhwan as much as he did. But he knew whatever Sunwoo would do, he would deserve. So, he smiled to himself, before he opened the door and headed inside on his own. 

Only ten minutes prior had Jinhwan entered the living room to see Solar lounging with her best friends and siblings. She was laughing away on the couch at something someone said, and he found himself smiling as he walked to sit beside her. For a moment, she seemed free. And he really hoped she really would be soon. He hoped it would all go okay, that it would all be over. 

"Hey, Nani," she turned to greet him once she felt his presence and his familiar scent entered her nostrils, "did you have fun?"

He still felt uneasy about firing a weapon, but he didn't want to tell her that - or anyone, for that matter. All he did was return her giddy grin, shrugging, "it was a new experience."

"I never really got used to it," she admitted, "Sunwoo taught me how to shoot every weapon there is back when we were teenagers, but I never used one unless I really had too..." she trailed off, the familiar memories riddling her brain. 

Jinhwan's mind went somewhere else. Somewhere that he was still thinking about fairly often. "Hey, Soo, I'm sorry about what I said still - I...I really regret saying that to you."

She just shook her head, waving a hand in the air in dismissal, "it's whatever, Nani, it's the best for both of us if we just forget about it, okay? Emotions control everyone during hard times."

Jinhwan was thankful that she was so warm hearted. He was lucky to still have her in his life. He had to remind himself that everything he would be doing would be for her, and whatever happened, he wouldn't let anything happen to Soomi. He promised, and he knew he had to keep it. 

Suddenly, they'd all heard heavy footsteps, causing some of their heads to jerk in that direction. They'd assumed it was Sunwoo and Jungkook, but were unsure as to why the footsteps were so harsh and heavy. 

That was when the gang leader had rounded the corner into the living room, grabbing the collar of Jinhwan's shirt and throwing him against the wall in a split second. He was so fast that it took an entire minute for anyone else to react, including Jinhwan himself. It was like Sunwoo was minutes ahead of everyone else, like he thought at incapable speeds. 

"Sunwoo, what the hell?!" Solar had screamed, rushing to her feet.

But she just wasn't fast enough, as the gang leader had aimed his fist, swinging and hitting Jinhwan straight in the nose. It was a heavy impact, a heavier one than Jinhwan had ever felt in his life. It was like someone had thrown a truck at his face, as he slid against the wall and onto the floor once Sunwoo let him go, cupping his nose with both of his hands and squeezing his eyes shut to try to ease the insufferable pain. 

He knew it wasn't worth trying to defend himself, and he wasn't an idiot. He knew what caused this and who had told Sunwoo. 

Solar had been in the middle of cursing at Sunwoo about what was wrong with him, when the dark haired boy had finally snapped, "you're lucky I'm not doing more, Solar, after what I just fucking heard."

Solar's mouth slammed shut the second she heard those words come from his lips. She found herself gawking as the puzzle pieces put themselves together, and she turned in that moment, to see Jungkook entering the room. Their eyes met, and once Jungkook saw the look of betrayal on her face, he realized he probably made the wrong decision.

"I don't know why she keeps giving you chance after chance when you've done nothing to deserve them," Sunwoo had snarled down at the boy laying there, helplessly on the ground, clutching that custom gun of his tightly in his hand, "but there isn't a bone in your body that deserves her. I will be watching you, and if I find out you speak one more fucking word like that about her, I will not hesitate to track you down and kill you."

"Sunwoo, I don't need you to -"

" -Solar doesn't have a sick bone in her fucking body," he'd finished with a sigh.

The brunette's attention behind him had suddenly gone astray. Her phone had began angrily going off, buzzing away over and over as if she was getting a million text messages per second. The thing was, Jinhwan's phone, that was beside him and had fell out of his pocket, began doing the exact same thing. 

Sunwoo had about to have said more, likely make more threats, when Solar's broken voice caused everyone's attention to switch. 

"Oh my fucking God."


179:00 | capturing a story

Sunwoo had left Jinhwan alone after saying it was a warning. He'd tried to look over Solar's shoulder, to ask her what the hell happened that had her so worked up, but she pushed him away. Jungkook had walked forward, seeing if maybe she would talk to him, but he knew she wasn't very happy with him right now, either. The brunette had just sighed, not even making eye contact with anyone else. All she'd done was step forward, grabbing Jinhwan's hand and helping him up.

The boy didn't look at anyone else, either, as the two headed for the door like they were runaways. Chanhee had been looking in confusion, and him and Chaerin rushed over to the girl before she could open the apartment door. 

"Sol, excuse me, do you mind telling me what the hell just happened?!" Chanhee had snapped at her under his breathe so only they could hear. 

There was a pained look in her eyes, she looked overly stressed for a reason he was unsure of. After a second, she sighed, shaking her head in dismissal, "just turn on the news, Hee. We'll be back."

That was all she said, before she broke her wrist out of his grasp and left with Jinhwan by her side. 

Chanhee raised his hands to rub his temples as he turned to look at everyone. They were all staring back at him, as if asking if he found out anything relative to what they just saw. The tired best friend had just shook his head, making his way into the living room and grabbing the remote. Everyone was silent as he pressed a few buttons, flipping to the local Korean news channel. 

"Another one?" Jacob had been muttering in shock.

There it was on the screen, pictures of Jinhwan and Solar, plastered all over the media. There were pictures of them together in their apartments, the studio, holding hands on dates, in the car together. There seemed to be pictures of them from every era of time, even dating all the way back to 2019 with Solar holding alcohol bottles and laughing by a concerned and tired Jinhwan. 

It was an outbreak, a new scandal that everyone would be eating up. But the problem was the fact that it was coming out only a week after the Jungkook scandal - which meant every news media was slandering Solar, assuming she was seeing the two at the same time, that she was a two timer. It was all over each news headline, right there on the television screen. 

Kim Soomi Caught With iKON Member, Kim Jinhwan, Dating Back To Shortly After The Scandal Of 2018 Involving BTS' Jungkook. Has Kim Soomi Been Two Timing? 

The headlines got even sillier than that. Chanhee had let out a deep sigh, burying his face in his hands as he muttered curses to himself. As if this couldn't get any worse, here they were.

"Interesting he would decide to release this shortly before the big day," Sangyeon had been observing as he watched the pictures flash on the screen. 

Sunwoo had been standing at the entrance of the living room, arms crossed over his chest, eyes narrowed at the TV screen. To him, this wasn't personally a big deal, but he knew Solar had explained the harshness of the industry, and how the idol's were expected to keep a pristine and innocent image. He had a safe idea that her being seen being romantic with two different idols, along with pictures of her drunk, would probably not be the best for that image she held onto. 

And he couldn't believe the audacity.

"Sangyeon, call Hyunjoon," he had demanded sternly into the silence, tired of listening to the obnoxious news reporter on the TV ramble. 

The boy already knew what he was saying, and his eyes were wide as he looked to the leader in question. Nonetheless, he picked up his phone and immediately dialed the number, holding it to his ear to call the members that were out at the warehouse where their hostages were. 

"Kill one," he asserted without a breathe, "the youngest, send a picture to the number we were contacting him through."

"Hold up, you're doing what now?!" Chanhee had been asking in bewilderment.

Sunwoo rolled his eyes. "Nothing that concerns you, pretty boy."

And then before any of the siblings or friends could ask more questions, he spun on his heel and walked away, likely back to the balcony where he could freely think. 

Meanwhile, Solar had been staring intently out the window as Jinhwan drove his car back to the guy's apartment. It probably wasn't the best idea for them to even be going back to an obvious place for paparazzi, nonetheless with each other, but she didn't care anymore. She figured she didn't have much to lose at this point. 

Solar wasn't really worried about what their CEO would say. He knew he wouldn't be that surprised by now, surely, but he would never have the guts to get rid of two idols that made him so much money. The thing she was worried about what the media and how it was likely ripping her apart right now.

People had grown to like her and Jungkook's relationship. She still received a fair amount of hate, but they were tolerated. She knew that now that a scandal with Jinhwan had leaked, she was getting called ungodly names, vulgar things she didn't want to imagine. 

Wanting to just disappear, she lightly hit her head on the cold window out of frustration. Jinhwan hadn't said much since they got in the car, his nose was still bloody and hurt like a bitch. He just figured that she didn't want to talk about it. More so, he hadn't paid attention to his angrily vibrating phone, either, figuring it was just his friends being annoying.

With that being said, he had no idea that what happened involved him.

"Uh, Soo," he cleared his throat, glancing to her before looking back to the road every few seconds, "do you - do you want to talk about it?"

Solar almost wanted to yell at him for the fact he didn't already know. How could he just ignore his angrily vibrating phone, not even pick it up once? But she realized it was just because he was enjoying the moment with her, he was enjoying her company so much he didn't care what his phone was going off for. 

Her brain yelled that to her when she looked to him. Poor Jinhwan, Jinhwan that she'd adored since the day she first saw him, heartbroken and lost. Since the day he found her, drunk and destroyed, and promised he would help her through her alcoholism. Since the day they first kissed, deeming they would stay friends but friends with benefits, friends with benefits that turned into something she didn't plan for. She never planned to fall in love with him, Soomi had been tired of love. She was tired of being hurt, tired of trusting people. 

She'd been hurt by him, but she hurt him, too. They still held that love for each other when their eyes met, she could feel it, feel it tickling her heartstrings, coursing through her veins. But it was different now. She had too much going on, too much riddling her brain. She had too many feelings for too many people. But she could say one thing, she wasn't getting back together with Jinhwan nor Jungkook. 

Not because she didn't want to, but because it wasn't safe or right. Because she had a feeling that this Friday would be bigger than they all expected. 

With a deep sigh, her green eyes tore away from the inquisitive Jinhwan, looking down to her phone that she'd picked up from the cup holder. Jinhwan had entered the parking lot of his apartment complex, parking in the usual spot before turning off the car. His eyes watched as Soomi stared down at her screen, pursing her lips. There was a look of sadness in her eyes as she sighed weakly, "well, they're ripping me apart, Nani."

He stared at her for a second, processing what that could mean. He had heard her say those words before, and once he realized, he snatched her phone out of her hand to peer down at it himself. 

That was when he saw all the headlines, all the headlines about iKON member Kim Jinhwan and female solo artist Kim Soomi. 

"Holy fucking shit..." he muttered in disbelief as he scrolled through the pictures that were released to the media. They even had photos from when they took their road trip to Ilsan-Ri, that was over a year ago already. 

"Yep," she dully agreed, leaning her head back against the leather seat, "I don't think our CEO would care, it's just the fact everyone is calling me a two-timing whore."

Jinhwan frowns as he looks to Soomi. She was hurt by what everyone was saying online, and he had seen her that way before. It fueled him with rage, rage because this time he felt partly at fault since it was involving him. "I'm sorry, Soo," he was shaking his head in anger, trying to think of anything he could do to help, "do you want me to release a statement? I can tell people -"

"- No, Nani," she chuckled at his innocence, sending him a small smile of assurance, "it's fine, really. As long as they don't release anything else about me before Friday... it's - it's whatever."

He knew that wasn't true one bit. "Soo, it's not whatever, I know this isn't good for your health or your career."

"Course it's not," she snorted, "but there's nothing I can do about it."

Before he could say anything else, she'd grabbed the handle of the car door, opening it and climbing out of the vehicle. Jinhwan was sat there, awestruck for a minute as he watched her walk towards the apartment complex.

He heaved out a deep sigh, looking down at his phone screen, where he was receiving endless DMs and messages about the dropped scandal. The thing was, no one was really mad at him. All the hate was towards Soomi. It wasn't right, it wasn't right because none of these strangers knew the story, knew the truth, knew what she was going through.

And she was right. There was nothing they could do about it. 

Feeling frustrated and hopeless, he groaned out loud, slamming his head back against the seat. "I'm sorry, Soo..."

The brunette had already been entering the apartment of the iKON members. Junhoe had been the one who opened the door, and his eyes had widened at the sight of the train wreck that she was. Though, he had already heard the news. 

"Well, didn't expect to see you here," he welcomed, "especially not after that fucking nightmare."

"Soo, oh my gosh, how are you feeling?!" Donghyuk had been crying, rising from the couch and rushing towards her to give her a hug, "I'm so sorry, you really don't deserve to deal with another scandal so soon."

She didn't really care to hear anyone's words of sympathy. All she did was shrug, "yeah, it's whatever... it's not my first rodeo," she mumbled, confusing the boys as they didn't expect such words from her. Her green eyes were fixated on a certain door down the hall, and she turned to them to blankly ask, "is Hanbin in his studio?"

They exchanged another glance, pursing their lips. 

"He's out with his girlfriend," Donghyuk informed her after a second of thought.

Solar had nodded in acknowledgment, already walking towards the door as she called back to them, "Great, tell him I'm using it."

Junhoe had wanted to follow her and bother her about what she was really thinking or feeling, but Donhyuk had grabbed his arm, whisper yelling to him that she likely wanted to unwind with music. 

Which was exactly what she wanted to do. 

Solar was tired. 

She wanted it to be over, more than anything. She wanted to see that man dead, the man that kept fucking up her life, kept hurting people she loved. She was tired of cowering in fear, living in concern that another secret of hers would be told to the world. 

Recently, she found herself wondering if it was a bad idea for her to become an idol. She wasn't made for a strict place like this, a strict life like this where someone was watching your every move. She had too many dark secrets, too dark of a past. And she'd tried to run from it, and it'd worked for a while. But you could only run so far from a past so dark.

A deep exhale left her lips as she fell onto the chair in front of the equipment, her eyes wandering as she turned on Hanbin's laptop that he used to make music. 

One thing she had found in Korea was her passion, her therapy. 

And it was the only thing that could help her that night. 

She'd picked up his guitar that he kept hanging in the corner of the room, strumming a few different chords to try to get a feel. Around ten minutes, she seemed to feel something, something coursing through her veins. It was something she had been thinking of for a while now, something that would tell her story. 

In around half an hour, you'd see her there, leaning over the desk with a pen in hand, scribbling on paper even though she had an entire studio around her. It just felt natural. 

This ain't for the best
My reputation's never been worse
So, you must like me for me
We can't make any promises
Now can we?
But you can make me a drink

Dive bar, on the East Side, where you at?
Phone lights up my nightstand, in the black
Come here, you can meet me in the back
Dark jeans and your Nikes, look at you
Just think of the fun things we could do

She'd dropped her pen after a while of scribbling out words and rewriting them. She felt like there were parts missing, like she wasn't capturing what she wanted to perfectly. Solar wanted to tell a story, a story that captured the three boys that had changed her, that had helped her. The three boys she loved in their own ways, that were such different personalities that she could never let go of. The three boys that lived in her head even when she didn't want them to. 

Is it cool that I said all that?
Is it chill that you're in my head?
'Cause I know that it's delicate
Is it cool that I said all that?
Is it too soon to do this yet?
'Cause I know that it's delicate

180:00 | the ending

There were a lot of things Solar Lee would have done differently in her life; a lot of decisions she would change, a lot of roads she would turn on. All the decisions in her lifetime, whether she still agreed with them or not, had come together to make her the person she was today. The girl that had been through thick and thin, hell and back, that was somehow still standing there, alive and well - at least on the outside. 

The last day at Jinhwan's apartment had been a blur. She hadn't done much but stay holed up in Hanbin's studio, writing, mixing, and recording music until she felt like her heart was content - or more so, until the boys had dragged her out of the studio against her will. The only time she slept was when Jinhwan insisted she take a nap, which she had for a few hours. Then, they'd all sat around the apartment, talking about what would be happening - what was supposed to happen after the math. 

They'd tried to comfort Solar to the best of their ability, but even Junhoe's idiotic sense of humor didn't help. She just felt blank, exhausted, drained of everything she had to offer. Solar was tired.

She'd called and spoke with the staff at her company, and they agreed to allow her to halt her current activities because of the drama that the scandal had caused. None of them really seemed mad at her, they just assured her that they would do damage control and take care of the hate she was receiving online, and that she could return when she felt ready. 

Solar wasn't sure when that would be. 

It'd taken some work to leave the apartment complex safely without any paparazzi seeing them, but they had. And it would bring them to the day of, the big day, sitting around her new apartment. Sunwoo had been up and about since they'd got there, making sure everything was in place, that they had all the gear they would need. Most of the gang members had already left, as they were instructed to be at the location ahead of time, still watching over the left hostages. 

The brunette had yet to hear about the ones that Sunwoo had killed thanks to the scandal's the stalker had decided to pull. He could careless about murdering them, personally. If it was what it took to show him he wasn't playing around anymore, to warn him to stop, he would do it in a heartbeat. 

"Is everyone clear of their roles and positions?" Sangyeon had been asking everyone, trying to make sure they all felt confident. Really, he was just talking to the siblings, Jungkook, and Jinhwan. Chanhee and her other friends decided to stay at the apartment, just for the sake of saving space - and the fact they had no experience with gang jobs, so it was for the best. 

They already had enough inexperienced people on their team to deal with. Sunwoo was already unhappy with that, but he figured they might as well attempt to help if they wanted to so bad. More firepower was usually a good thing. 

Solar had been fairly silent since they got there that morning. Her brain felt jumbled, likely because of the fact she barely slept, and the fact today would be one of the biggest days of her life. There were so many things that could go wrong, and she kept replaying them all in her head. It was bad enough that she almost lost Sunwoo, she didn't know what she would do if it happened again. Would he be able to slip out alive again this time? Unlikely. 

Not to mention, now all of her friends lives would be on the line. All of them would be there, standing with her, protecting her, risking their lives for her. She was thankful, but it made her feel bad. 

She hated how she'd brought such a poison onto Jinhwan and Jungkook's lives. They were once so innocent, at least in her eyes. They'd never shot nor seen a weapon before, they'd never tasted death, they'd never seen the kind of vulgar things that really happen in the real world.

It must be nice to live that way, in complete ignorance, shielded from all the bad. She could almost remember what it felt like, before she really knew it all, before she learned about everything that really went on. It was all around her - death, murder, fraud, crime - it had always been around her, she just never knew until she focused. 

And she was never the same after that. 

"Suit up, sweetheart," Sunwoo hummed from where he was standing before her on the couch, chunking what appeared to be some kind of special bodysuit and a bunch of bulletproof gear on her lap. She squeaked out in shock at the sudden weight of it, it was ungodly heavy. 

She didn't even get a chance to say anything to him, though, as he'd began giving out the gear to everyone else. A lump formed in her throat as she watched them all putting it on, getting ready for the day ahead of them, it didn't feel real. She just hoped nothing went wrong.

With a sigh, she rose to her feet to put hers on, too. Sunwoo had finished passing out the gear, and was making his way over to all the weapons they'd had stored in her apartment - which were all no doubt illegal. 

Her footsteps creak against the wooden floors as she stood behind him. He knew it was her, taking something out of his pocket and holding it out to her. 

She found herself confused as she took the familiar weapon from his hand. "Wow, Sunwoo, giving your special gun to me?"

The gang leader had rolled his eyes as he stood up, holding a rifle in his hand, "Yeah, Sol, don't accidentally shoot yourself, okay?" He was teasing her right back. It made her smile even though it was mean, just because she was glad he could still joke with her on a day like this. He was the only one who could, everyone else was too nervous, looking like they were about to go off to war and they'd never see their families again.

There was a tint of happiness in her green eyes as she looked up to him. "Thank you, Sunwoo," she felt herself getting sentimental.

He looked down at her with a blank expression. His eyes raked over the others in the room, taking in the reality of the situation. After a moment, he professed, "I love you, Solar," his hand reached up, the other still gripping the rifle, fingers soft against her cheek as they ran across her jawline, "it'll be over soon, sweetheart, I promise you that." His voice was nearly a growl, it was intimidating, dark. 

She didn't get a chance to say anything more, because he turned and walked away. She listened in to what he was saying to everyone for a minute, arranging how everyone would be travelling, going over the plan, their positions, what to do if it went wrong. Her ears had turned off, and she found herself standing there in the corner, in deep thought. Solar didn't really have a job, she was lucky that Sunwoo was even letting her go. But she knew he was confident in protecting her, she just hoped nothing went haywire.

"Are we clear?" the gang leader had been asking everyone sternly, his eyes dark and ready to kill as they looked over everyone. "I don't want any amateur moves, if you want to help, this is your time to not fuck up," his eyes were on Jungkook and Jinhwan as he spit those words. They were harsh, and the two had only hummed in agreement, knowing how serious this was. 

With that, the gang members that were still there began to grab all their equipment, leaving the apartment to go load it into the van they would be taking. Chanhee had made his way over to her, a rather stressed expression on his face as he looked to her in concern, "Solar, are you sure you want to go? It's really not necessary - I don't understand why that idiot would even allow you to go."

She expected his parental lectures, causing her to only sigh in dismissal, "Hee, I'm going - it's my fight, remember? I'm not going to let them all risk their lives while I sit around and do nothing."

The boy rolled his eyes, annoyed at how stubborn she always was. Knowing there would be no convincing her, he turns to find Sunwoo, only to see he was making his way over to them already. 

"If anything happens to her," he pointed an accusing finger to the mafia leader, "I swear to fucking God, you will have a horrible day."

His threat meant absolutely nothing. But Sunwoo still rose a brow at him, "Right, I'm sure scared of you," he scoffed, "as if I'd let anything touch a hair on her head." With that statement, he pushed the hair behind Solar's ear, before putting an earplug device inside her ear. It shocked her, before she realized it was what they used for communication.

Chanhee had been glaring lasers into the dark boy, before he looked back to his suited up best friend. "Stay safe, okay? I don't want to see anymore death," his eyes glanced back to the boy beside them, "unless it's you... again."

"Touching." 

After her childhood friends had gave her a hug and wished her well, they'd gotten out of the way so everyone could gather what was going on and prepare to leave. Solar pursed her lips as she made her way towards Jungkook and Jinhwan, who had been sticking near each other - shockingly. Though, it made sense since they didn't quite fit in anywhere else.

"Hey," she greeted them both, causing their eyes to bolt to her instantly. Clearing her throat, she nervously bounced on her heels as she offered, "you guys really don't have to go, you know, there's no telling what'll happen. It feels wrong for both of you to be risking your lives for me, I know you're not accustomed to this kinda stuff."

Jungkook had stepped forward to pull her into a big hug, catching her off guard, before she slowly wrapped her arms around his torso. 

"Don't be silly, Mi," he assured her, "I'd do anything for you, you know that, I just want you to be safe."

"Thanks, Kookie..."

"It's only right for us to help," Jinhwan cut in, sending a side glare to Jungkook as the two had broke away from the hug, "we owe it to you, Soo."

She sent him a small smile but shook her head, "you guys don't owe anything to me, okay? I dragged you into this."

"Hey," Jinhwan told her sternly, "we dragged ourselves into this. Stop blaming yourself, it's not your fault. Everything will be fine."

His words were comforting, and she pulled him into a hug, right there in the middle of the living room. Everyone was probably rather confused why they were all hugging as if it was the last day they'd ever see each other, but you never knew. They had no idea what would go down in such a short amount of time. Solar wanted to make sure they all knew how much she appreciated them, just in case it was ever too late. 

"I love you, Soo," Jinhwan had whispered in her ear, kissing the side of her cheek before they pulled away. 

It felt different hearing those words from him on that day. She couldn't think of the last time she had, and it made her nearly tear up as she looked up at the caramel boy. She was glad he was here, she was glad they made up, that he was back in her life. 

Sunwoo had cut off their sentimental time by calling out that it was time for them to go. Solar sent one last smile to the two boys, before she jogged after Sunwoo, who was leaving the apartment with her siblings. 

"Oh, my big sister!" Chaerin had been crying dramatically as they all entered the elevator, wrapping an arm around her shoulders and pulling her in for a side hug, "you dumb bitch, to think this mess is almost over, and then we can all go home."

She knew that when Chae said that, she was including her. Solar couldn't even think of home right then, because it did sound like a nice place to be. 

"Don't celebrate too soon," Jaehyun warned, leaning against the side of the elevator with a sigh, "it's not over till it's over."

Chaerin rolled her eyes. "Yeah, cause you're suddenly so poetic."

There went the bickering. It never stopped, even on a day like this. That's how it is when you come from the Upper East Side, this is just another normal day, another gun shot, another life lost, all normal. 

The elevator let out a ding, and seconds later, they were all leaving the apartment. The van that everyone but Sunwoo would be travelling in was waiting right in front, and they'd all waved to Solar as they climbed inside. 

Sunwoo had no idea yet, but she had been planning to ride with him. 

"Sol, I told you to ride with them," the boy had sighed as he heard her footsteps running up behind him. 

But the van was already driving off behind them, meaning there was no choice. "Too late, I want to ride with you!"

He wasn't surprised by her antics as they'd stopped in front of an unfamiliar motorcycle. Obviously, none of them had their vehicles since they were in a completely different country across the world. So, all the vehicles they had were likely rented or stolen. Solar's eyes had widened as she looked at the bike, it was shiny and new looking. 

Though, there wasn't time for her to gawk at his new bike. With a deep breathe, she climbed onto it behind him, wrapping her arms around his toned torso.

At the end of the day, Sunwoo didn't really mind. Deep down, he was happy she wanted to be around him instead of either of those idiots. There was a smirk on his lips as he revved the engine, before they'd drove off down the road, following the van with everyone else.

The drive there was almost peaceful. Sun beaming down on their skin, wind blowing through her hair, her eyes tired as she laid her head on Sunwoo's back. It was comfortable, in a weird way, comfortable enough that she almost forgot about the reality of what was really happening, only until she realized they were no longer in the city of Seoul anymore - but somewhere that looked deserted, abandoned, in the middle of nowhere.

Since talking wasn't really an option, all she could do was watch the way their surroundings changed. The roads turned to gravel, and there wasn't another car other than them and the van in sight. It felt like they were entering an alternate reality with the way the atmosphere changed. It would always be fascinating how dead parts of Korea were once you left the safety of Seoul. 

The motorcycle had come to an abrupt stop, Sunwoo using his foot against the ground to halt it. She'd taken the time to look up at where they were, seeing a giant warehouse in front of them. It was rusted, looked as if it was built decades ago, but somehow still standing. 

Sunwoo got off first, taking off his helmet and shaking his head to get his messy hair out of his face. After setting it down, he held out a hand to Solar, who took it, following his actions. 

But he didn't let go of her hand as they walked towards the warehouse. There were already a couple vans parked out front other than the one the others had travelled in, since there had already been some of the gang members here. Sunwoo's eyes darted every which way, scanning their surroundings, as he took out one of the weapons he had on him just for safety. 

They'd made their way to the back of the van Sangyeon had been driving, where everyone was grabbing their weapons. Since Sunwoo had gave her his custom pistol, she figured she would be fine. She hadn't really been planning to do any of the killing, even if she wanted to be the one to put a bullet through his head, but then again, you never know.

Jungkook and Jinhwan had been the last ones to grab theirs. She could see the nerves inside of them as they held the illegal weapons, turning to see her. Each of them gave her their own assuring expressions, as if they were trying to telepathically tell her it was alright. But she could tell they were scared, that they were having second thoughts. None of them really knew what was about to happen, none of them knew what they were going to see. 

But they knew they had to be prepared for anything that might come to them. And they would be, because they were doing it for the girl they loved. 

"Come on, he should be here soon," Sunwoo had urged everyone to get in their positions, but didn't move from beside Solar as he waved them all away.

"Good luck, you guys," she waved to Jinhwan and Jungkook as they were walking off to a place she didn't know, "just stay focused, okay? Be safe!" She kept calling out, feeling nervous for them. She didn't know what she would do if anything happened to them, and it didn't feel right for them to be here right now. She hated that they were so stern on coming. 

"Love you, Mi!" Jungkook had yelled back, and she shook her head in disbelief. 

At least he was finding a way to smile.

"Alright, now or never, Sol," Sunwoo hummed, grabbing the girls hand again while he held a rifle in the other. He only let go for a moment to make sure it was loaded and all he'd have to do was press the trigger. 

With that, they made their way into the warehouse, with some of the gang members trailing behind them with their own weapons. She felt like a bodyguard, like she was the President or something. It was weird, with the way they would crowd around her, guns up and looking every which way, prepared to shoot on sight. 

You're used to this, she told herself, this is nothing. 

It would all be over soon. 

There was a foul scent when they entered the warehouse, and she knew it better than she wished. The sight of a dead body had entered her eyesight only moments later, when she saw a bright looking Hyunjoon that was standing next to the hostages - the only thing was, some of them were dead. She already knew it had been from Sunwoo's likely warning to him since he'd dropped those scandals. 

She didn't want to feel bad, but she still dead. So much so that she closed her eyes, turning away from the sight of the horrified and starved human beings.

Sunwoo had done some kind of sign language to the members, and some of them had rushed off to different positions. Her brother, Chaerin, and Haknyeon were standing around Solar, continuing to guard her and keep an eye out for any sight of who they were supposed to be seeing today. 

"Do you really think he's gonna show?" Chaerin had been asking out into the silence, the only sound besides her voice being the harsh slap of the wind against the warehouse walls. "God, what if he fucking stands us up?!"

The gang leader was not in the mood for small talk. He stood there, gun in hand, in front of Solar with his eyes narrowed, watching each inch of the empty warehouse closely, "Quiet, you never know what kind of entrance he wants to make..." his voice was low, low enough so only they could hear, but also so his earpiece would pick it up.

After another minute, he muttered lowly into the earpiece, "is there any sight of movement?"

In a few seconds, voices from Jungkook and the other gang members that were stationed elsewhere responded with a no. 

They were all left there, confused and on guard. Solar didn't move a muscle, her green eyes just raked across the cracked and old concrete flooring of the warehouse, searching and searching for the familiar man she once looked up to. Never would she think she'd be in such a position, with her family again that were risking their lives for her, or with Sunwoo and his gang members, the boy she never expected to see again after 2013. Yet, here she was, standing there with them around her, hoping that this wouldn't be the last day they'd all spend together, hoping that nothing would go wrong for them this time. 

It was so quiet you could hear a pen drop. So quiet, that it gave her goosebumps, made her want to crawl into one of those cracks in the ground and hide. She wanted to be strong, she should be strong, but whether she would admit it out loud or not - this was scary.

Click, click, click. 

The sound of footsteps made everyone raise their weapons. The sound of them all loading their guns echoed through the giant building, and she held in a scream at the sight of the man entering from the other side of the warehouse. 

The thing was, he appeared to be alone. He walked at a slow, casual pace, as if he was entering a café for a cup of tea, having a nice stroll down the street. She found herself wondering if it was because he had something else up his sleeve or if it was because he knew the fate that would be coming to him. 

But like Sunwoo said, it likely wasn't the second one. 

The gang leader's eyes were glaring into the man, and he began to walk forward so they could meet in the middle. Solar debated with herself for a minute, before she took out her own gun and jogged to walk beside him. This hadn't been part of the plan, and her sister had whisper yelled for her to come back, but she ignored her. She still wanted to be a part of this, this was still her fight, it was all about her. She couldn't let Sunwoo stand around and do all the work himself. Solar didn't want to be that helpless. 

"Well, well," the man chuckled out darkly as they had both stopped before each other. He was barely recognizable to her at that point, he looked like an entirely different person, dressed in dark, dirty clothes - with the most menacing expression she'd ever seen. It made her feel sick to her stomach, just seeing someone that had caused her so much trouble, "to think you really slipped out alive, a pesky one - at that..." his eyes had slowly moved to the girl standing beside him, and Sunwoo had half stepped in front of her as if to shield her if he tried anything, "and Solar, little evil Solar, I take it you didn't enjoy the world finding out about your disloyalty?"

"You're talking to me, not her," Sunwoo growled, causing the man's eyes to snap back to him after a moment of staring at the girl like she was fresh meat, "this ends today, whether we come to an agreement or I put a bullet through your head. You have nowhere to run, this entire place is surrounded, so I suggest you make a decision now - and quickly - because I've been itching to put a bullet through your head."

He stared at the leader for a minute, as if he was taking in his expression, reading his mind. That was, until he laughed, he laughed for what felt like an hour, his chest bouncing up and down as his eyes crinkled. Until he shook his head, "oh, Sunwoo, you are clever, but it's a shame you just aren't clever enough," he'd sighed with fake sympathy. There was a weird humming sound coming from the air, one that had everyone looking up at the sky in awe and concern, trying to figure out what was going on. "You're father did warn me about you, about the training you underwent at such a young age - quite impressive, I might say."

Wait...

Sunwoo's expression showed the faintest amount of shock. Solar, on the other hand, felt like she was going to pass out. Did he just say Sunwoo's father? 

His father...

The hidden card. 

The trick up his sleeve.

Sunwoo's father.

Before either of them could say anything, the loud, spitting noise of a helicopter was heard. Sunwoo had grabbed Solar's arm, pulling her as they took steps back, because it was landing right in front of them through a large hole that was in the roof of the abandoned warehouse. 

No one knew what to think right then. Solar wanted to hurl, she wanted to scream, she wanted to do so many things in that moment as the helicopter had whirled to a stop, and the doors opened to reveal a man she had not planned to see today.

But nothing could compare to how Sunwoo was feeling. His eyes fumed with rage as his hands gripped the rifle in his hand tighter, and he'd slammed his feet against the ground as he walked full speed towards the two men standing there. 

Until, a bunch of armored men had stepped off of the helicopter, following his father. They stood beside him, as if he was this beloved human they had to protect. Sunwoo halted his steps feet away from him, his body trembling with anger as he spat, "You have some fucking nerve showing up here, working with this fucking joke of a human being..."

It wasn't all he had to say, but his words had trailed off. 

"Son, did you really think he could've done any of this on his own?!" His father laughed loudly, purposely exaggerating on purpose, "Of course not. Behind every villain is a mastermind, don't you know that well enough?"

It made sense, Solar realized, it made more sense than she wanted to admit. That was how he knew so much, how he found out so much information so easily, how he had access to so many kinds of technology to allow him to do the things he did - all the pieces fell into place. Sunwoo's father had shot her when she first went back to New York, when he realized she was "back" with Sunwoo, as a warning. He had wanted her dead since the first day he'd saw her. The man was one of the richest business men in the United States, but also one of the most violent and dirtiest. He could freely do whatever he wanted and all authorities turned an eye because he was so rich, he paid everyone off and continued to live freely. 

It all made sense...

"And Solar..." His father had heaved out a disappointed sigh as his eyes switched to the girl, "I warned you, didn't I? You just never learn, it was quite my luck when this one came to me about how he wanted you dead." He bellowed out a laugh again, sounding like a cringey villain from a children's TV show. Solar felt sick every time he opened his mouth. 

"I'd never let him do it on his own, though, it's always been a dream of mine to watch you die."

The sound of a gun cocked and loaded was heard. Solar thought it was only Sunwoo who had raised his weapon, only until she turned to realize that everyone else had, too. Her siblings looked nearly as angry as Sunwoo, clutching their guns in their hands as if they had no fears. The gang members sported blank expressions as they aimed their weapons at the enemies, knowing that the most important thing for them to do that day was end it - even if it meant they had to kill them all. 

But they wouldn't shoot until Sunwoo signaled to - or unless he shot first. 

"Did I not make it fucking clear to you the first time, old man?!" Sunwoo snarled, not caring that the armed men next to his father had their weapons raised right back at him, "I told you to never step foot near her or it'd be a real bad day for you. And you have the fucking audacity to work with him and plot to kill her?" A chuckle of disbelief left his lips as he recited it out loud, unable to process the guts this man had. "You think some fancy men would stop be from putting a bullet through your head? Come on, old man, are you forgetting who I am?" 

The gang leader was taunting, teasing. He was a snarky, cocky, but professionally trained to fight anyone in the world. The problem was, the man who put him through all that training was who he was aiming a gun at. And he hadn't learned his demeanor from just anywhere, he learned it from his father. 

"No, son, I think you're forgetting who I am," he retorted, crossing his arms over his chest and raising a brow as if to challenge the boy before him, his own child, "you still work under me, whether I allow you to run your own operations or not, you still respond to me, son, the one who raised you - the one who gave you all of this in the first place."

Sunwoo's grip on the weapon only tightened. It was likely he was debating what to do, what move to make. He never expected to be in this position, not today. He never expected his father to do this to him, even though he should've. His brain was busy weighing out the different options, what would occur if he pulled the trigger and sent a bullet straight into his father's head. It sounded like a great idea, only except for the fact there were several armed men surrounding him, ready to gun him down at any moment. 

And it would almost be worth it, if Solar wasn't right beside him. 

"I have detailed evidence that I will gracefully submit to the FBI regarding one of the most wanted gang's in America," his father voiced, the darkest smirk on his lips as his eyes overlooked all of the gang members he could see, in their different positions across the concrete flooring, "this kind of information will send all of you to jail indefinitely, and leave me profiting." 

It was insane.

He was willing to throw his own operation under the bus. The only reason Sunwoo lived this life, the only reason the gang ever started, any of the dirty work ever occurred, was because he raised him to do it - he forced him against his will. He made him live this kind of life, it was the reason he was born, it was the reason he existed. And all of it was essentially for him, for his dirty business that he refused to run ethically. 

And he was willing to throw it all away, throw all of them under the bus and have them sent to jail for life, but leave himself clean - all for this. 

"Whatever kind of offer your about to make," Sunwoo growled, his tone as warning as it could be, "you better make it a good one."

He had to stand his ground, even if he wouldn't have much of a choice. The threat he made was a serious one. Sunwoo couldn't allow all of them to go down like that. 

"Get in, come home, I have several jobs for you to attend to that need attention immediately," he motioned to the open helicopter, "though, you don't have much of a choice in this matter, unless you'd like to spend the rest of your lives in jail." 

Solar's eyes were wide as she glanced from Sunwoo to his father. She knew what was going through his head, and she knew that it wasn't a good idea right now. 

"What about Solar?"

His father rolled his eyes, shaking his head in disbelief as he mocked, "Solar... Solar... always about Solar. There are more important things at hand, son, your job and your life."

But the boy didn't care for his words. "I'm not stepping foot on that fucking plane until you guarantee that this is over for good," he demands, eyes glaring to the man that still stood beside his father, "until this fucking idiot is gone, and I know she'll be left alone. Then, we can talk work."

Sunwoo's father was clearly not impressed or happy with his demands. Still, he heaved out an impatient sigh, allowing the man beside him to speak up, "Where are my family members? I'll need to see them first before I even think of not killing that dumb fucking -"

"- Call her one more thing," Sunwoo dared, taking a few steps closer to him with his gun high in the air, straight at his head, "Really, I dare you."

The man had put his hands up, and with that, Sunwoo had backed off. He turned to look to the gang members that had the hostages, and had covered them with cloth so they didn't see them. He made a hand motion to them, signaling for them to bring them over. 

The members had began to pick up the tied up hostages, carrying them towards them to show him. 

Once they'd reached Sunwoo, they dropped the few people on the floor like glass, right behind him. The man's eyes were filled with horror, but you could tell he was trying to keep himself together. Sunwoo had pointed a gun to one of them that was laying there on the ground, weakly, looking as if they could barely move, "so, do we have an agreed upon deal or no? We'll go back to the States, he can have his family back, and Solar can live her life normally and freely - but if I so much as hear that a picture of her leaks online, I'm coming, and I will kill you and any trace of your bloodline." 

This wasn't initially how the plan was meant to go. Originally, Sunwoo had high hopes of putting a bullet through that mans head. But with the extra additions, it was suddenly not as possible as it had been before. 

It wasn't the way he wanted this to end, but if it meant Solar would be safe and alive, he would just have to deal with it. 

Meanwhile, however, Jinhwan and Jungkook were both feeling mixed emotions from where they sat on the roof, peeking over through one of the many holes in the rusted, broken down building. It wasn't a very comfortable place for them to be, but they felt like it was a good position to see what was going on in case they needed to intervene. 

Jinhwan had been growing concerned by the looks of what was going on. The only way he could really see was by looking through the scope with the gun. He could hear some of what was going on since they all had ear pieces in, but there was a lot of feedback from the mics. 

He'd gathered that the plan hadn't quite gone as it was meant to, though, and that Sunwoo's father had shown up. 

"Looks like they're making a deal," Jungkook muttered, using his own gun scope to watch what was going on. 

"I wouldn't trust that guy to follow it..." Jinhwan voiced his own opinion, keeping his crosshair over the stalker man that had once been her manager. He felt like there was something off about this. Would a man like Sunwoo's father and a guy that had sworn on killing Soomi really just let off with a deal so simple? Would they really come to a peace agreement? It didn't feel right.

"Sunwoo's got in under control," Jungkook had insisted, trusting the gang leader fully, "definitely isn't his first rodeo, you know." 

Jinhwan ignored Jungkook, knowing he was just being snarky like usual. The last thing he cared about doing right now was arguing with him. Instead, his eyes squinted into the scope, noticing a certain something about the man he hadn't seen before. 

Every once in a while, his hand would itch towards his back, like there was something hidden there. It was subtle, so subtle he didn't think Sunwoo would be able to notice it from the front. But he could see there was a certain something hidden in the back of his jeans, something that he clearly desperately wanted to grab. 

A gun.

It looked like they were talking somewhat peacefully, but yet the man's hand kept itching and itching a little more towards his gun with every word that he spoke. From what he could hear, it sounded like he wasn't feeling too happy with the agreement. And it made sense, because he wanted Solar dead. It had probably been part of his plan the whole time, that if he saw her, he would find a way to kill her - even if it meant sacrificing himself. 

He knew it. 

Solar was standing right there, in clear sight of him. The other gang members were either getting on the helicopter or were still by the hostages behind Sunwoo. He was the only one who knew what was happening right now. 

He had to do something.

Jinhwan felt a lot of emotions about everything that happened. He hated Jungkook, he hated the audacity he had to be the way that he was. He hated how he had the guts to pull out a gun at him, as if he would ever pull the trigger. He didn't particularly like Sunwoo either, but he could admit he somewhat deserved being punched. What he did, what he said to Solar wasn't okay and he would regret it forever. At the end of the day, he appreciated the way that boy loved Solar, how he would do anything in his power to protect her. He'd taken a literal bullet for her, and it was more than Jinhwan could say for himself. So, no matter how he felt about him, he owed thanks to him. 

As for the man, the man who had done so much to hurt all of them, to ruin Solar's life, he knew it in that moment, he knew he would almost be happy to send a bullet through his head, to know he was putting an end to all the havoc he caused. He remembered, he remembered how he asked Solar if she ever felt remorse for the people she killed, and she'd said no, because they deserve it, because they aren't good people. And those kinds of people, they poison the world around them, they make it more vile and unlivable with each day that they continue to exist. 

He understood her now. 

And he promised her, he promised her that he would end it. He would end it someway. He would step up, he would stop being so helpless, and he would put a stop to the pain that this man kept causing them all. 

That was why Jinhwan raised his finger over the trigger, loading the gun and making sure his shot was perfectly aimed over the man's head. 

"What the hell are you doing?!" Jungkook had cried out to him in shock, not understanding what he thought he was about to do, because he hadn't been paying attention to what the man down there was about to pull out of his pocket. 

And there was no time for him to explain, because in seconds, you can lose someone. In seconds, he could've pulled out the gun and shot Solar. Which was why he made sure he pulled the trigger first.

The bullet was a clean shot, a shot sent straight through the head of the stalker that had ridiculed Solar for so long. Blood splattered through the air, cutting off any conversation they were having. There was almost a moment of silence, a moment for everyone to process what just happened. 

Sunwoo's face was blank, as blank as it always was. But there was a seething rage underneath his irises, one that was pissed someone had just done that and caused what he knew was about to happen. 

"Down, get down!" Men had screamed at Sunwoo's father, hurriedly crowding around him and ushering him back. 

Sunwoo grabbed Solar, covering her the best he could as they ran to get behind the van away from the gunshots that began to fly. 

"Sunwoo, what do we do?!" one of the gang members yelled to him from behind their cover. 

The leader was breathing heavily, listening to the gunshots hit the metal of the vehicle. He didn't have time to think, but he didn't need to.

"Shoot," he monotones back loudly enough for them to hear, "just fucking shoot."

Bullets flew. 

They flew until men ran out of ammo, until men fell flat on the ground, their bodies lifeless. The family members of the stalker that was now dead had been killed off with him, so there would be no witnesses left to tell the tale of what happened that day. 

It was sad. It was sad the way things played out. 

But maybe that was how it was supposed to end. 

Solar witnessed a lot of death that day. Death of friends she cherished, friends she never planned to see again, friends she loved despite their ill flaws. She saw deaths of people that could be argued as innocent, people that probably had lives left to live. 

And like always, she felt like she was this useless being. This shadow that couldn't fight back, fight back against the one man who had wanted to ruin her all of her life.

Sunwoo's father. 

"Enough!" Sunwoo's father had screamed out, the word echoing through the empty, abandoned warehouse, "Enough! Look how many we've both lost, son. Listen to me, get in the plane and come with me. It'll be done for then, you know there's not much time until those pesky authorities show up, and I won't wait much longer." 

His father didn't really care about the deaths, they didn't mean anything to him. 

Sunwoo knew he still wanted Solar dead, deep down. Even if he claimed she would be safe, he could never believe him. He'd tried to hurt her, kill her, more times than he could count. 

He walked out in the open against once the gunshots stopped and the last of his father's men had been called off. He didn't know at the time, he didn't know how many of his members he had lost. He didn't know the damage quite yet. 

But he knew an end had to be put to this, he knew that no more death could occur. And more so, he knew there was truth to the words his father spoke. 

The authorities would no doubt be here soon if they weren't already. 

"You're right," he hums, walking to stand only feet away from the man who raised him, "we don't have much time." 

"Great," his father took that as an agreement, motioning to the opened helicopter, "let's go then, I assume the rest of your friends will find their way perfectly fine." 

But he had other plans.

Other plans that consisted of him raising the gun in his left hand and sending a bullet straight through his father's chest. 

Dead.

His body fell limp on the ground, and the last couple of men were next.

Dead.

Everyone in his father's crew, every accomplice.

Dead.

There was death everywhere you looked. 

"Sunwoo..." Solar had whispered, walking up behind him. Her hand was shaking when she placed it on his shoulder. There were words that needed to be said, but she couldn't seem to find them. 

All she could do was wrap her arms around him from behind and hug him with every ounce of strength left in her body. 

She didn't know then, either. She didn't know how many friends they had lost. 

She just knew that it was over.

Or was it?

Because would something as evil, as big as this was, would it ever really be over?

"Solar!" She heard the screeching voice of her sister, "Solar, this is bad, we need to do something quickly!"

"I hear sirens, I don't think we've got much time," Jaehyun had spoke through Chaerin's screams. 

"What is it?!" Solar was calling back, rushing over to where her siblings were. 

That was when she saw it, she saw all the dead bodies. The dead bodies of people she loved. 

"We need to go... now..." Sunwoo had been quick on his feet beside her. He had to be experiencing the same difficult emotions when he saw them, too, but he didn't show it. He never showed it. "Get them all on the plane, now!"

The ones alive didn't waste any time. They hurriedly loaded up their friends bodies and any life left in them onto the plane. 

She just watched, tears streaming down her face, realizing the seriousness of this day. 

She'd never forget it.

"Wait..." she muttered, "Jinhwan... Jungkook..." 

The sirens got louder and louder.

They got so loud, so loud she realized they were right there, zooming into the abandoned warehouse, tires screeching against the concrete floor. 

"Solar, get on the fucking plane!" Jaehyun was screaming. 

But there were people missing. They might still be on the roof, but she couldn't seem to spot them from where she was standing. 

"I can't!" she screamed back, choking back her tears. "I have to find them!"

But it was too late. 

"Police!"

"Down on the ground!"

She was done for.

All she could do was raise her hands as she was surrounded.

"Go!" she screamed, "just go!"

And they listened, because if they didn't they would've gotten caught, too. 

She watched the helicopter fly into the air, the police began shooting guns at it, as if it would make it come back. 

But it was gone. 

Would they find them? She didn't know. 

She hoped they didn't. 

She hoped they'd be okay now. 

Because she wouldn't.
